title: 2025/07/31 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-07-31 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Briefing on the National Infrastructure Plan
topic: Briefing on the National Infrastructure Plan
description: Thursday 31 July 2025 Transport and Infrastructure Committee Briefing on the National Infrastructure Plan Hearing of evidence: New Zealand Infrastructure Commission, Te Waihanga Raveen Jaduram, Board Chair Geoff Cooper, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the National Infrastructure Plan. New Zealand Infrastructure Commission, Te Waihanga. Raveen Jaduram, Board Chair

chunks.text: How many people do you want to come up? I mean, if feel got more, what more of the team coming up to the table, then, then do that. We, we'll probably call 'em up as, okay. That'll work here. Okay. Well, um, Ravi, Jeff, team, um, great to have you all here. Um, thank you very much for the, um, uh, for the draft plan. Um, much a good read.

Um, we, what we're gonna do is hand it over to you to start with and, um, then questions I've told, um, I've asked people where they've got questions. I said I've got about two hours worth, but, um, we'll go from there. Okay. You're the also Thank you. Okay. So over, over to you. Thank you. So, yeah, uh, I'm ravine chair of the commission, chief Cooper, CEO.

And thank you for the opportunity. No one. Thank you. Um, I'll just get onto the draft National Infrastructure Plan. Uh, it presents a step towards building national consensus on New Zealand's infrastructure challenges we face, uh, well-documented demographic pressures from an aging population with infrastructure investment capacity, constrained by about five to 7% of GDP.

According to current fiscal parameters, critical knowledge gaps exist in infrastructure, asset management, government and partners must improve the understanding of existing asset condition, lifecycle requirements, and strategic replacement schedules.

Our strategic approach must prioritize optimizing existing infrastructure capacity and performance before permitting resources to new construction projects. This will ensure we get maximum value from our current public investments. The draft plan delivers transparency regarding New Zealand's infrastructure needs, associated costs, and funding pressures.

Systematic improvements in infrastructure, project delivery is also essential. Deferred maintenance and replacement programs have significantly undermined public trust in New Zealand's infrastructure delivery capabilities, and threatened future consensus building efforts.

We have already conducted considerable public and stakeholder engagement on development of the plan, including ongoing cross-party briefings. We expect this to be a useful input to helping establish greater political consensus for, for infrastructure.

The delivery of the national infrastructure plan remains the focus for our organization through to December, 2025, and will be followed by a government response in mid 2026. So with that, I'll hand over to Jeff. Thanks, ravine. Yeah, I just to, I'll just make a couple of comments, um, and then we can get in into questions.

I think if there's sort of three things I would want you to reflect on with this plan. Um, the first is that when we have put forward what we think is a level of expenditure that we can afford as a country, we have first and foremost started with what, um, what it means to be sympathetic to the household balance sheet, right?

That is where we have started. We haven't assumed that there will be new funding tools coming along, and we've had a long, you know, decades long conversation about potential new funding tools. When we see those funding tools change, we will change our, our forward guidance.

And so we're starting with what we can, what we can see right now. We've noted in there that New Zealand currently spends, uh, among the top 10% o of the OECD, the amount we spend on infrastructure. We are also, um, trying to show folks that when it comes to New Zealand's infrastructure, um, when we look at it on a per capita level basis, a a different story actually emerges.

Uh, we are the same size as greater Sydney in terms of population, but we are spread over a landmass that's 21 times larger. When we start comparing our assets, uh, to places like that, the, the story actually looks a little bit different, right? So we have sort of six stadiums of capacity over 30,000.

Um, Sydney has five, right? In New Zealand flies to about 20 airports around New Zealand. Sydney has two, two airports, right? So when you start looking at per capita stocks, uh, a a slightly different story emerges. I think that that helps us understand levels of affordability and what we can expect. We go further with that to understand what a new capital budget actually looks like, a non-renewals, new capital budget, right?

And we, um, the, the detail there is that New Zealand over a 30 year period has in the order of $125 billion to spend on new services. That helps place, uh, why I think it is difficult sometimes to get big projects across the line, not necessarily because we can't get political consensus on them, but because in fact, the trade-offs of those very large projects, uh, are real and mean that we can't get services in other areas.

Um, or it might mean that, uh, user charges and taxes would, would need to be higher to, to do so. So the first one there is affordability. The second, uh, that Ravine has already alluded to is renewals. We as a commission are very fortunate to have been directed to think long term. Um, what that means is that we have to think through renewal cycles, um, how, uh, cities, communities are positioned to continually fund the maintenance and eventual renewal of the services, um, that they will rely on.

Um, we have said that of every dollar, um, that we spend on infrastructure, that some 60% should be on, uh, maintenance and renewals. Um, in the long run, when, uh, demand drivers are reduced, uh, that number will tend towards a hundred percent, right?

And my conversations with communities and areas, uh, that experience less population growth will tell me that the 60% number is far too low, right? Um, so in the long run, maintenance is almost everything. Um, the third thing that I would sort of highlight, um, before turning over to you, Mr.

Chair, is the role of demographics in the plan. Demographics are central, uh, to everything in the plan. They're central to, uh, the demand that we see for new infrastructure services. You know, one component of that is more people, more services and, uh, statistics New Zealand, uh, released its updated population projections, actually just a few weeks before we, we released this.

Um, but I think it's worth dwelling on this, that the, the low to high projections from statistics New Zealand put New Zealand, uh, at a population of somewhere between six and a half million and 12 million by 2078. That is an enormous variation. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Um, and that really focuses the mind on what the challenge is for asset owners.

That it's not always clear where the demand is when it will pop up. And if you get that decision wrong, it means carrying assets that can't, can't fund themselves or have to be funded from other other sources. And I can give give examples if you're interested, but the, the, the uncertainty there is real, and it is what asset owners talk to us most frequently about.

Uh, we have, um, areas of course that are growing, but similarly, uh, we have areas that are stagnating or projected to decline, right? Uh, this is less front of mind because growth infrastructure, I think oftentimes occupies the psyche. Uh, but 56 of the 67 territorial authorities are expected to either flatline or decline, uh, into the 2030s.

Uh, so thinking about how we manage services in a world where there are less funders around, again, is an area of, uh, immense concern, I think, and increasingly will, will become, um, top of mind. The reason why we are talking about it is because the assets that we build today oftentimes have, uh, asset lives of a hundred years that, um, that need to withstand these demographic changes.

The second part of the demographic story, which, um, is built into the plan is how we are aging, uh, and, and start contrast to the uncertainty that comes about from how much we will grow. Uh, our aging is far less uncertain. Um, we will go from a population of, uh, or from a, um, uh, state where we have seven working age people for every retiree in the 1980s to a world at the back end of this plan, where that number will be two to one.

Um, that of course has enormous implications for, uh, our fiscal capacity to fund infrastructure. Um, a point that is made right up front in the strategy. Um, but it also has wide reaching implications for the types of services that we require, uh, that relatively speaking, we will need more infrastructure for older populations than we will for, uh, for younger populations.

And this is fundamentally what is driving a lot of the recommendations through the, for guidance of the plan. Um, and of course, increasingly will be baked into, um, business cases, uh, for infrastructure services in the, in the future. Um, let me just end by saying that the draft plan is, um, firstly a draft.

So we're very much in full consultation mode at the moment. There is a legislative process with the minister, which goes for three months from, uh, from June 25, but we're also running a public consultation period of six weeks. Um, as, as well, the draft plan is not one that will sort of sit on, on a, on a bookshelf and date.

We are looking at this very much as an operational plan for the commission. Uh, and the various elements of the plan we will be updating, and I'll just quickly go over those. The Ford guidance, which is our assessment of, uh, capital investment requirements by sector. We intend to update, uh, every, every couple of years, um, as we get better, better information.

Um, the national infrastructure pipeline, which is, uh, uh, all of the projects that are in planning around the country, will be updated on a three monthly basis. We work with about 120 different organizations around the country to, to reflect, uh, what is in planning, and then the, uh, infrastructure priorities program, uh, which is the, uh, menu at our project level of advice and, and readiness assessment, uh, will be updated one to two times, uh, a year.

And on those three operational matters, I have, um, behind me, uh, the directors responsible for those. So Nadine Dodge is responsible for the infrastructure priorities program. Uh, Mac Kerr is the director of data science and analytics who runs the national infrastructure, um, pipeline. And, um, uh, Graham Campbell, uh, the director of, uh, research and economics runs the Ford Guidance Program, uh, as well.

And John Butler we have here who's been coordinating, um, the, the plan. So with that, I'm happy to hand back to you, Mr. Chair. Thank you both. And, um, thank you for such free and frank, um, uh, advice to start us off with. Um, I'm gonna keep, we've try and keep the questions quite tight if we can, just picking up on the first one of 'em, because as you've come before and, um, we're talking about demographics and how central those are to the plan, I've sort of said for 40 years we should have a demographic strategy of some sort.

Um, how, If we Said, I mean, that range, you've got between sort of less than 5 million and 12 million, um, in terms of being able to plan anything for infrastructure without some sort of greater degree of certainty, how difficult is that? And secondly, how much of what we are pro what what you are thinking about in terms of going forward.

'cause you talk about 125 billion to spend on new services that, well, that will obviously depend on how many people you're, you're dealing with, whether it's 5 million or 12 million or something in between. Um, what proportion of, um, expenditure do you see being necessary for growth? Yeah, if we are five, presumably virtually none, um, if we're at 12, presumably it's a heck of a lot.

But how do we, yeah. How do we work on that, that sort of, Yeah. So, um, on, on your, on your first point around, um, uncertainty, um, there, as, as I, as I said before, this is like front and center for, for all, all asset owners, there are different types of uncertainty, right?

Um, and I would sort of differentiate between two, particularly the first is a question of timing. When is the growth going to come, um, uh, such that we might need a service? Um, and so, uh, you know, I'll take the case of Auckland where historically our population projections have been for the last 20 years.

It's not true overall in fact, but just for the last 20 years have been, um, undershooting. Where Auckland has got to this essentially is challenging asset owners to figure out when is the right time to bring the asset along. It is fundamentally a financing challenge because if you bring the asset on, uh, earlier than perhaps you, you should, according to demand, it means you might need to hold debt for a little bit longer, right?

So you've got a financing risk there. The second type of problem is if you get the direction wrong, right? So rather than growing your, uh, community actually starts contracting, right? And this is a much more serious problem. This is not a financing problem. This becomes a funding problem.

Uh, and if you have a funding problem, it means that the users aren't turning up to use the asset, right? Um, this is one that asset owners worry about far, far more in, in many ways. Um, so there are different types of uncertainty in, in different, um, sectors. Uh, I would point to something like where we see the most uncertainty is an area like, uh, corrections where our ability to forecast demand is not to be too flippant, like pretty hopeless.

We get the direction wrong all the time. So it's a, it is a type two problem. Um, uh, for for many other types of infrastructure, I would take something like, um, electricity where, uh, MBS projections have been wrong on the downside for a decade or or so. Um, uh, you know, that, that is a, a sector that's, um, that know the actual demand for electricity has been relatively flat.

So what to do in those situations. Um, I think where we point to in the plan is that buying, if you're not sure when demand is gonna come, buying options is better than buying assets. Um, leave the option to buy the asset, but do some of the initial work so that when the demand does come, you can turn it around quickly.

Um, to give you a concrete example of what this looks like, like land protection, right? Don't wait until moments before you decide you need the asset to go and buy the land. Um, buy the land, buy yourself some options, um, before that. Um, so that the implementing those strategies of investment under uncertainty is, is critical for, for infrastructure.

Um, your second question is about, uh, how our advice would change under different, particularly population growth estimates. We have, um, put forward a, uh, a expenditure number as a proportion of GDP, um, which of course is, is tied to, um, population growth.

So if the, if the, if the population were to grow less, obviously the capital budget would be less, but our advice of the proportion of GDP would remain constant, and that's actually why we've chosen that as a metric rather than, um, front footing with, with headline numbers, notwithstanding the fact that I gave you the, the headline number under the median just before, Um, I don't see any other hand.

Okay. I'll go to Julianne. Thanks so much. Um, I had a couple of different areas of questions. Maybe if other people have questions, like You can take one and then Yeah, yeah. Then unless there's in video else, I'll go one, and then you can have another one back again. Um, I was wondering if you could just expand on like, some examples of what using our existing infrastructure better before new capital investment and expansion might mean particularly in the transport space, please.

And, um, and I had a question about recommendations for change. Um, one of the recommendations for change is transport system reform, and it says land transport funding gap is closed by requiring user charges to fully fund planned investment. Would that be true of all types of projects or just certain projects? Because, um, given that there isn't full pricing for time of use or use of highways, um, you know, in most places there's some transport subsidy needed for public transport.

Um, I wouldn't think that investment in some big, well, some improvements to public transport could be funded from user charges, but mm-hmm. Yeah. So I was just wondering if that is applies to all transport or just certain projects? Yeah. Yeah. So the advice, maybe I'll start with that one and then I'll, I'll come back to the, um, making better use of existing infrastructure.

Um, where we are going with on that is that, uh, the, the, the, that the network itself should be able to pay for it for itself now, except in, in transportation there's questions of what networks, right? Freight networks, um, uh, eroding networks, CT networks, and so on.

Um, what, what we're saying here is that as a starting point that everyone using that network should sub, should, notwithstanding the fact that there might be cross subsidies within the network. And I would, I think this is an important point. So, um, if you think about the way electric transmission pricing is done, there are cross subsidies across the network, but the network is a whole pace for itself.

Um, it is, it is a lower bar than the bar that has held up to say, um, the airlines industry and the, and the airlines industry. Um, the bar is much higher. Uh, there we say, uh, essentially if you look at jet stars, um, uh, route coverage, um, you know, they will probably not extend new routes unless they can get a profit above zero, um, on that new route.

So for them, each part of the network needs to have a profit above zero. Um, what we are holding up for transport is less than that. That the reason why we think that this is important is because it creates pricing tension within the sector. Uh, and one of the most notable things about the transportation sector is that on a per capita basis, um, we have been spending, um, enormous, um, uh, amounts on transportation, uh, over the last two, two decades.

I think there is a, um, a chart in your pack comparing, uh, electricity per capita, um, spend to transport spend. Mm-hmm. I, from the top of my head, I think the per capita number for electricity is in the order of 50%, and for transportation at per capita number is 400% increase.

So we've seen a very large increase in transportation. At the same time, we have seen a sort of breaking of the ated model, uh, in transportation, and that essentially has, yeah, that's the one there, yeah. Has reduced the sort of pricing tension on the sector, um, uh, to minimize capital expenditure.

So part of this pricing, um, advice is, is about right sizing the capital, the perceived capital investment requirements for the sector, so that that areas of investment that have no chance of funding themselves off the asset base can still occur.

And I think that that's really I important part of this advice is that, yes, there is a, um, part of the plan that is saying we need to tighten up the user charger elements of, particularly for networked infrastructure, but the motivation for doing that is to release funding for social infrastructure because funding, um, infrastructure like hospitals and schools, um, correctional facilities, justice and so on, these are areas of the system that will never fund themselves.

Um, and so by tightening up the areas that can we, we release, uh, an ability to, to hit the infrastructure we need in the social space. Um, on your question about, um, how we go about doing that, I think that the pricing instruments in transportation are, um, you know, when you stack them up against how we do it in electricity, are found wanting, you know, we don't have congestion charging, um, in place.

The time of day stuff looks far more important. We're considering that later on. Yeah. Yeah. So, you know, so these are things that, um, we think will make a big, big difference, um, uh, in, in constraining the, the capital side of things as well. Um, does that answer Yeah, if, can I just do one follow up On if, if it's a quick one?

Yeah. Yeah. I then I, then I'll go just to, I, I, what I'll do is I'm not gonna worry about subs or anything like that. I'll just go ki and Carlos. Um, Dan, so far, I, I think as politicians and this committee in particular, we hear a lot from, uh, people in the sector, um, or in certain areas of the country who are saying, government, if you build us this road, um, we're gonna invest.

There's gonna be heaps of jobs, there's gonna be economic benefit, and the people who build those projects or saying, you know, we want a pipeline of these projects into infinity so that we can keep building them and making money. Um, would, what would the commission's advice be to politicians who are trying to take on board, you know, your expertise and advice versus that sort of like, lobby that's just like, just, you know, we believe us, if you build this, there will be economic benefits, even if you don't, you know, it's not gonna pay for itself.
And, um, there's, yeah. Yeah. Um, I think one of the answers to this lies in, um, my earlier comments around the renewal cycle, um, and being able to get through the renewal cycle. Um,

ultimately, you know, you want to build assets that have a high enough usage of them that the users are then able to pay for the ongoing upkeep of the asset, um, uh, in, in the future. So I think in the, in the plan, we asked this question noting that there are fiscal constraints.

I think a natural question is to ask, well, when can we go outside of those fiscal constraints? Because the truth is those fiscal constraints look actually, um, they're quite confronting. Mm-hmm. Um, uh, when you look over a a 30 year period, I mean, we are running structural deficits that get us to a number of 150% net core crown debt to GDP.

I think that's concerning. Um, there's obviously some policy questions there that go outside the, the realm of, um, of infrastructure, but we look at that and and say, well, if that's the case, trying to hit our numbers of somewhere between five and 7% of GDP actually looks pretty challenging in that context.

A natural question is to say, well, how can we go outside of those numbers? And there's many different types of infrastructure that actually will not be flustered by those fiscal constraints at all. Um, regulated utilities have their own, um, have their own funders. And so, uh, you know, if they can put forward high quality projects that people are prepared to pay for, actually that doesn't, the fiscal stuff doesn't matter so much, um, to that.

Um, so when it comes to road to, to transportation, um, the, clearly there is a much tighter link between, um, the asset and, um, fiscal constraints more, more generally, which I think become relevant. So our advice to this is if you want to go outside of those, um, those fiscal constraints in the first instance, you could do it, uh, within, um, base load, but it means, it probably means getting less social infrastructure, at least you wanna raise taxes, and that's the choice.

Um, if you want to go outside of that and do, um, uh, say an economic PPP or something that where the asset is gonna pay for itself when it comes to, uh, the, uh, the roading example, but more generally the process, we wanna show, what would you need to see to believe that this road, uh, or asset is gonna be able to pay for itself.

Um, and in the case of roading infrastructure, we show that, that we would need to see costs, uh, that are below $32 million per kilometer usage that's above 40,000 users per day. Um, and, uh, what's the last one? Cost, usage, and thank you. And travel time, which is, um, greater than 15 minutes.

If you manage to hit those three things, your, um, the willingness to pay the toll will cover the asset, um, and the ongoing maintenance costs, and it gets you outside of your fiscal constraints. So I think that's the question you can ask is if, if this is true, not withstanding the initial stimulus, which is entirely a separate issue, right?

The, in the initial stimulus comes and goes. And I have been reflecting on the, the recent, um, coverage of Y me debt, which I think is an interesting one, right? The stimulus has come and gone, the project's been built. The question is now, can you fund the project through its operations and maintenance? Uh, and if you don't have enough users, you know, we've seen what happens.

Mm-hmm. Um, okay. Ki then Carlos, then Dan, thank you. Um, thanks to you and your team for coming in. And I wanna acknowledge the briefings that you've given us leading up to the announcement of the, the launch of the draft. Um, I found the conversations that we've had incredibly useful to understand your intentions and what you have to get from this.

Um, so I'm gonna ask this question in the context that we've had plenty of chats before, but I, I've been interested in, um, uh, some of the things you've indicated that you talked about how your observations in the draft are on the assumption of current funding tools, which I can understand why you do that.

Um, but then we've also talked about scenarios that you've, uh, labeled that indicated to financing would be challenging. Fiscal constraints are quite confronting. Um, once we go through this process, public submissions, the government reports you settle on a final report, consciously said that it will be fluid.

Do you think you'd ever be at the point where you might be able to indicate if, uh, the funding tools were different, what could be achieved? So you sort of touched on that from a user pay perspective there. Mm-hmm. But you also indicated that, uh, if, uh, government taxation doesn't change, I, I'm paraphrasing here, then we're gonna be limited in what we can do.

So these are legitimate debates, but they're debates that can often get politicized. Now, as an independent agency, uh, is there a role that you could play without pushing for a particular avenue Correct. To indicate this is what we can do under current settings, these are what we could do if things change, these are the options that could fill that void.

Yeah. Um, so where, where I would sort of go to on, uh, the, on the funding issue is that a lot of this comes back to project quality, right? That if we can demonstrate we have good projects that have users that are willing to pay for them, all of a sudden, um, the funding looks kind of different.

I think where we've often got tripped up is that the projects themselves are unable to satisfy that condition. Um, and if we're trying to, to put forward projects that don't have enough users, we're always gonna end up throwing our arms in the air and saying, we've got funding problems. Um, that just seems like a natural place to, to end.

Um, what we are trying to do through the infrastructure priorities program is, is really do just that. We are looking to, um, uh, put forward a menu of options that can say actually like, here's a set of projects that potentially could pay for themselves, or, um, here's the, the, the things that we would need to see to believe that they could pay for themselves.

Right. Um, let, let me, let me give you an example. Just like a, a sort of corner example of this. Um, in, in Norway, they're building a, a road from, um, uh, from Norway through to Germany. It's a prefab tunnel, right? Um, it's very expensive, billions of dollars. Um, but it's got a high usage 'cause people are going from, uh, one busy city to another busy city.

And this role, uh, this, this tunnel is generating enough revenue from the tolls not only to pay for the capital cost and ongoing maintenance costs o of the, of the tunnel, but also is enough to start funding hospital infrastructure. In other words, the quality of the project is so high that it solves its own funding.

It solves its own funding problem. Um, and I think fundamentally, if we're looking at projects that create funding issues, we need to ask questions about, uh, costs. Can we do this cheaper timing? Do we need to wait until we see more users for the project? Um, you know, and, and other things like, like regulatory issues and whatnot, but how to bring that down to an area that actually does make value and eases some of the, the funding things.

So that's, that's, that's the test that we're running through the infrastructure priorities program. So in time, I would hope to be able to come to you with a, um, you know, a longer list than just the 17 that were released a, a month or two ago and say, um, you know, here are some projects that, that make this easier for you as a decision maker.

Carlos? Yes. Just, just refer some of the information you give to us. I mean, um, you definitely use United Kingdom Germany as a, as a, as a sample. Yeah. But, um, I can see all your page. Is that on? I don't know what patient. Okay. They don't have a patient. At least we can see which one it's carry on is. Yep. So, um, I can see all your recommendation are based on the Germany model.

So can, can you, can you, can you tell you expand a bit more, a more information why you use you United King income as, as a, as a comparison here and, and what's going on with them as well, with the way they invest in their, uh, the Sure. Well, um, I mean the, the genesis of that chart really was a, was a conversation that we had with the folks from the, the UK who were grappling with this idea of pipeline certainty.

Mm-hmm. And, um, you know, the chart demonstrates, um, I think electrification of the rail network and uh, uh, one country is taking a very much on off approach to its, um, Its investment into the network. And the other is taking a much more programmatized approach.

Um, I think, you know, our motivation for using that chart was that there are some things, uh, that, that have a low amount of uncertainty. We know we're gonna have to do them, right? And the, the plan goes some way towards saying, you know, the starting point of this is maintenance. Um, but there might be other things as well, um, where we should really be able to start programatize it, um, and smoothing this off.

And that would be a win for the sector. Um, because, you know, um, uh, pipelines ultimately, if they can be built up of, um, things like not contracts from NZTA, which are, um, very steady and very certain, um, this starts helping the sector understand what its pipeline looks like.

Um, and I think with, you know, the, the motivation for using this chart is to say that it's been done before we, we can do it. Mm-hmm. And also, I think there's another, another, another figures you show me that the later on, on the report as well, um, this one time of use price and fraud.

So based on the, based on the, based on the diagram you showed me, there's one in forecast disappear. So can I understand more, like, so where's the risk of the people? What, what's happened are using, uh, some other form of public transport, or are they, um, you know, just go to the, to the city at different timing or, or like what extremists, you know, where, where the affect the, the, the, the city center or can can give us a little more information?

Yeah, I, we can, I, I'll have a first go at this and I, Nadine, if you want to add to this feel, feel free. 'cause Nadine has done quite a lot of work looking specifically at, um, at the Stockholm case. But I think for a start the picture, um, there is, uh, uh, a provocation because there's a lot of cars on the left and not many on the right.

Sure. The first thing I would say is that, um, uh, you don't have to take many cars off the road to go from gridlock to free flow because the marginal cost of the next car actually gets really, really high. So that's, that's the first point. The, the, the second point is sort of, um, you know, where have these cars gone?

Um, and in that instance that was looking at a peak, um, period of time. Um, so some of those cars have, uh, have gone into the inter peak, right? Um, earlier or later, which is important from a capital investment point of view because for roading networks, a lot of the time we are investing for peak.

So if you can smooth that peak, it actually means you buy yourself a lot of future capacity and, and can avoid, um, capital investment. Um, some of those people will have gone onto public transportation networks and other modes. Um, and, and then others, and I think this is probably one of the more, more interesting ones that we don't think about all that often is that they will avoid the trip altogether.

Um, and uh, and this is what's quite interesting to me, right, is that by putting a, a charge in place, you might actually just see some trips stop altogether. And those are, um, you know, maybe it is the case, um, that rather than going to a, um, chiropractor, you know, a few suburbs away, that instead you just say, okay, I'm just gonna go to the one round the corner, right?

And it's those micro decisions that a charge is asking consumers to make, and the reason that we're asking them to make those decisions is because it's a very costly time to get on the network. Um, so Nadine, I don't know. Yeah, that was good. Yeah. Okay. You can follow up with the specific version.

This is the businesses or retailer being affected in those areas. So certainly in, um, I can, I can comment on New York, it's a different city altogether, just for different, but certainly they have seen the opposite effect, right? There is, You see the business are doing much better because they got this car going into the Yeah. They've seen more people, people traffic as a result. Dan, Hey, thanks Mr. Chair. Thanks, uh, to you and the team for the plan.

Two questions. The first is, so you've got this project pipeline that you've established all the projects and the dollar value, and it looks very daunting. It's kind of like, where do you start? How do you fund it? Um, what I, what I hear from you is that you're gonna kind of go away and say, here's the high value ones out of that list.

And what I hear from you is that actually it's high value for foreign or not foreign investment, high value for private investment. Is that what I hear from you is that high value ones that probably could be, um, funded by private investment. Mm-hmm. So I'll, so the difference between, so there's two products here.

Um, the first is the national infrastructure pipeline, and we are not making any judgements on what is in the national infrastructure pipeline. We are simply going around to, uh, to all of the organizations that do capital in investment in New Zealand. And we are asking them to tell us what their intentions are, um, where the projects are at, and planning how their intending to fund them, what level of certainty they have, um, and, uh, and when we might see this coming up for, say, procurement, so that we can signal those things to the market, um,

uh, that is a tool that we are using, uh, as part of our legislative mandate to coordinate the sector so that folks can see, um, what is going on around them. Um, and, uh, the coverage of that has increased dramatically. I, I don't wanna get ahead of myself here, but I think that New Zealand might have better visibility of its infrastructure planning than almost any other country in the world right now.

The reason why I say that is because, um, you know, we've obviously looked at other, other pipelines. We think we've got about two thirds to three quarters of all capital expenditure. Um, as a way of comparison, the UK re re-released its national pipeline a couple of weeks ago.

They had circa 550 billion in their pipeline. We have, um, we have over 200 billion in ours. Um, so proportionally the size of a country, um, those numbers should proportionally be somewhat more similar. The differences we, um, are talking to a, a, a much wider group of, um, organizations.

So I think there is some confidence that you're starting to see like a very decent picture of all of the planning that's going on. So I think that that's a, that's a step in the right, right direction. Um, the IPP is different. The infrastructure priorities program, this is a voluntary program. So, um, a agencies, the private sector elect to send us their projects that they have in planning.

Um, we then, um, uh, well, there's a triage point of this, so it has to be nationally significant. So we're, we're sort of, we're not doing the sort of 10 and $20 million ones. These are the sort of more, more big projects. Uh, and then we are essentially stewards over an independent process, right?

So the business cases will come to us, uh, we will either evaluate them internally or in some cases, if we don't have the expertise, we will, um, get an external reviewer. That appraisal will then go to an independent group of five, uh, expert panel who will make sure that the appraisal was done in line with the evaluation framework we set out and is published on our website.

Uh, and we'll assess the project against, um, three criteria, strategic alignment value for money, um, and deliverability, uh, and, uh, and then give it a, a stage of readiness, right? Stage one being, yep, we agree you've got a problem, but we don't think you've got a solution just yet.

Stage two is you've got a problem and you've got a range of potential solutions. And stage three, uh, which is investment ready. And the intention here is to provide a bit more vis visibility to both decision makers today, but actually because infrastructure has such a long life to it, and we all all know that planning takes a long time, this is, is just as much a signal to future decision makers as well so that they can understand where a project is at.

If a project is at stage one, which is that we think that there's good consensus, they've got a problem moving quickly to a funding decision, um, and, and executing shovels on the ground, you can expect time delays and cost overruns because the project isn't quite there yet. You can't avoid the need to go through these things.

Um, and so, uh, it's, it's, as I said before, our project level of, of advice on the readiness of the project and, um, what, what the next stage is sort of likely to be right at the moment. Um, uh, we, we released, uh, round one of that, um, and there were the 17 projects, um, that have been endorsed at various stages.

We will be running this process, um, once or twice every year, um, to give you an idea of, um, you know, where we might end up. It, it took Infrastructure Australia that runs a similar process, took them sort of three to four years to build a mature list of projects. Um, so I would orders of magnitude expect that length of time before we see something that's more mature.

I don't know if, uh, Matt or if Nadine want to add any comments to that pipeline or IPP if you've think I've covered it. No, that's pretty good. Okay. Yeah. Awesome. Thank you. Um, the other one is more drilling down to the time of use pricing. Um, curious, you've just come back from New York understanding the New York, um, implementation.

Uh, this committee is considering the time of use charging. It'll just be curious on any kind of lessons learned from your observations over there, uh, as we prepared to kind of deliberate and discuss how do we set up the framework for New Zealand? Yeah. Um, firstly, the commission is very encouraging of this work.

Um, uh, I think we've said in the past that this might be the single biggest thing we can do to improve, um, infrastructure services is getting transport pricing, uh, right. And the plan sort of speaks to that, the strategy in 2022 in some ways, um, went into more detail on congestion charging, um, the, um, well, I was in, uh, New York with the minister a couple of weeks ago to, uh, understand more deeply the outcomes of the New York congestion charge.

So thank you for the, for the question. It's not often you get to see a new congestion charge put in because these things are not done very often, and I think these are probably one of the more difficult urban policies to get in place. Um, as a general comment, I would say that the policy has outperformed what was expected of it, um, which again, is a surprise.

So often I think we're, um, you know, we don't always, the policy doesn't always kind of achieved what was intended, but in this case, it's actually achieved a lot more than what was intended. So the, the traffic time, time delays have dropped by 25% in Manhattan. I used to live in New York, um, uh, in the 2010s.

And, um, it was not uncommon for, um, you know, for roads just to be completely backed up. Um, I, I would never take an east to west bus in Manhattan because you could literally walk faster, uh, than, than the bus. Um, this has all changed quite dramatically. Uh, the, um, the bus system which works on the east to west, um, AXI is now functioning.

It works. Um, they're looking to improve services and get more, um, uh, throughput on that. Uh, the, um, travel times in the CBD have I increased by 15%, um, and travel times over the river crossings have declined by six to 42%. Um, so these are quite big numbers.

Um, what is interesting in terms of, well, actually a couple of other things that, uh, are tangential to, to the main story about, um, moving some cars off the network for faster flow noise complaints within the zone dropped by 45%, which is quite a big deal if you live in New York, very loud city. Um, and crash related injuries are down, uh, 15% as well.

Um, which the MTA when we talked to them about said these things, we need to make more of these, these things. Um, um, on a approval rating ratings, clearly this was a difficult scheme to get in, in place because you've got city level, state level, federal level, and aligning, um, the politics between those three levels is, um, actually far more difficult probably than, um, than New Zealand.

We have a very streamlined system. That was something we heard a lot over in New York. Um, but, uh, what is interesting is that approval ratings for the scheme have started just as we saw in Stockholm, um, have started to outweigh opposition, right? So there's more people that approve of the scheme than, than disapprove of the scheme.

And interestingly enough, the approval ratings for private vehicles coming in from outside of the city has increased as well. And that has been one of the most important, um, changes in the last three months because it has somehow quietened the resistance to congestion charging if, um, you know, once the, the private vehicle owners see that they can generate some pretty serious time savings, um, that's an important sort of constituency.

So what, what, what lessons from the MTAI point to a couple of things. The first is that, um, the MTA was very good at setting up a, um, real time data analytics unit to coincide with the congestion charging. Um, it is mission critical to get the findings of congestion charging out so that you can shape the, or provide the evidence base of what's actually happening.

Um, they reiterated the need, the need to do that. And so I have been encouraging that that is done, um, that we can get the evidence fast so that people can, can see the benefits. On, on the, on the flip side, I think the MTA, um, was, um, very, um, constrained by the fact that the scheme was written in some detail into legislation.

Um, and so they feel quite handcuffed in some ways that they can't iterate the scheme. Um, I, I have sort of given the advice of don't let the perfect be the enemy of the good when it comes to scheme design. You're gonna find a lot of naughty issues in there. Um, uh, you know, don't get held up by those issues would be my advice.

They, because their scheme is written in such detail, they actually would have to go through quite arduous processes to change the scheme. Um, and, uh, my sort of advice would be flexibility is key and, and the ability to iterate is key. Um, also the ability to change the price, uh, so that you maintain the travel level savings is important as well.

And who, um, you know, who, who has autonomy over that price. And so, again, the model I would look to would be autonomy with appropriate economic regulation in place. Uh, the congestion charging scheme in New York starts off at $9 and we'll go up to $15 over the next, uh, five to 10 years. Thanks Very much. Yeah, That is Very, very helpful. Yeah. Thank you.

Where central government has, uh, a bigger balance sheet to actually probably, I don't wanna say hide, but maybe conceal some of the, some of the, um, um, infrastructure, um, asset practices that actually, 'cause we, we make the point here that in some ways local governments, asset management practices look ahead

of central governments practices. So if you look across all of the TAs, they're all at different stages. Auckland's trying to deal with like, how big are we gonna be? Um, where are we gonna grow? All of these stuff, these things, rural pay, who district council is thinking, how on earth do we maintain our assets? Um, and rural pay who doesn't look different, they look ahead of the curve, right?

Um, and that is the message I think to, uh, to all of, um, new Zealand's TAs, is to think very carefully about how many assets you need and what you can maintain in perpetuity. Um, and to build assets that have a high likelihood of, of being used. Um, so you know, these, these are kind of different messages to what you hear in, in the world of infrastructure.

'cause we are really looking at infrastructure from a micro point of view. How many users are there? What revenues being generated? Um, can you see yourself through a, through a renewal cycle? And that looks harder and harder for TAs that are, um, not generating growth. Another way of looking at this is that growth, population growth has allowed local governments to grow in ways that, from a financial point of view, don't always look very good.

Um, we published a report, actually Graham's report on this, looking at the fiscal, um, the fiscal upside of new population growth in different cities, and the extent to which that new population growth is able to cover the costs of the infrastructure to support them.

And, uh, the high level findings is that the new population generally contributes somewhere between 30 and 50% of all growth. In other words, growth seems to be weakening the balance sheet of the city. Now, my interpretation of this is that there has been a sort of local government, um, approach sort of, let's call it a, a grand compromise in local government where, uh, around the council table there is often a debate of, well, do we want to grow?

Do we actually want to grow? Because the district plans and zoning requirements actually make growth pretty difficult. That's the truth, right? Um, and so growth is, I think, contested at the local government level. Um, but a compromise is reached to say, okay, we'll grow, but will grow in areas further away in areas that don't have crowding effects.

They don't create neighborhood issues and so on and so forth. And the reality is that to service those areas from an infrastructure point of view is very expensive. It means longer roads, more pipes per household, more distribution lines. And all of that means that when you try and maintain those assets with not very many people, you're gonna struggle.

And, uh, and that is, it comes out, I mean, uh, the chair would know about this because Wellington is a great example. A hundred percent. Yeah. No, great, great questions and exactly the sort of things we're, we're thinking about as well. I think, um, I would start by saying we have a missing function in New Zealand.

We do not have asset management assurance. Um, we had, uh, uh, the crew out from Infrastructure New South Wales, um, who do perform this function. Um, and they essentially gave agencies a five year runway to improve their asset management outcomes.

And then they do rolling, I think 12 to 18 month reviews. And they have demonstrated that that has been a very useful process to improve capability and continue shining the light, uh, on, on those issues. Um, what is clear to us is that when you think about the different oversight mechanisms we use, or incentives that we use for people to maintain or agencies and organizations to maintain their assets, um, you know, if if it's private infrastructure, it's a profit motive, right?

If you don't maintain your, your hotels, people won't stay at them. This has been in place for about 15 years.

Um, and so, you know, we think that that could, um, require converting CO 23 9 into more of a legislative tool, um, perhaps. Um, but I think that there's some, some thinking to be done there.
